title: The --print Flag — Claude as a Script Tool

content.explanation:
By default, Claude Code runs interactively — you type, it responds, you go back and forth. But what if you want Claude to answer one question and shut up? That is what the `--print` flag (short: `-p`) does. It turns Claude into a non-interactive script tool: you give it a prompt, it prints the answer to stdout, and it exits.

This is the bridge between "talking to an AI" and "using an AI inside your programs." When you run `claude -p "explain what a Dockerfile does"`, the output goes straight to your terminal — which means it can also go into a file, a pipe, or another command. Claude becomes just another Unix tool, like `grep` or `jq`.

The `--print` flag also changes how permissions work. Since nobody is sitting there to click "Allow," Claude runs with no tool use by default in print mode. It only generates text. If you need it to use tools (read files, run commands), you explicitly allow them with `--allowedTools`. This keeps automated runs safe.

terminology.definition: A flag that makes Claude Code run non-interactively. Takes a prompt, outputs to stdout, exits immediately.
Standard output — the default destination where a program sends its text output.
A mode where a program runs without waiting for user input. It does its job and exits, usable in scripts.

codeExample.code:
# Basic non-interactive use
claude -p "What is a reverse proxy?"

# Save output to a file
claude -p "Write a Python .gitignore" > .gitignore

# Allow specific tools in print mode
claude -p "Read my package.json" --allowedTools Read
codeExample.annotation: First: prints answer. Second: redirects to file. Third: allows Read tool.

content.explanation:
Plain text is fine for humans, but scripts need structure. `--output-format json` wraps the response in a JSON object you can parse with `jq` or any language.

`--output-format stream-json` gives streaming JSON blocks as Claude generates. Useful for real-time integrations processing output as it arrives.

The most powerful option: `--json-schema`. Pass a schema, and Claude outputs JSON matching your exact structure. Define fields, types, and required properties — Claude conforms. This turns Claude from "generates text that might be JSON" into "generates validated, typed data structures."

terminology.definition: Makes Claude return its response as a structured JSON object instead of plain text.
Emits JSON blocks incrementally as Claude generates, useful for real-time processing.
A formal description of a JSON object shape — which fields, types, and requirements.

codeExample.code:
# Get structured JSON output
claude -p "List 3 benefits of TypeScript" --output-format json

# Parse with jq
claude -p "List 3 benefits of TypeScript" --output-format json | jq ".result"

# Typed output with schema
claude -p "Analyze this error" --output-format json --json-schema '...schema...'
codeExample.annotation: First: raw JSON. Second: jq extracts result. Third: forces output to match your schema.

content.explanation:
Unix pipes let you send output of one command into another. Pipe any text into `claude -p` and it becomes part of the prompt. Any text-producing command (`cat`, `curl`, `git diff`, `docker logs`) can feed directly into Claude.

Piped content arrives as stdin, prepended to your prompt. `cat error.log | claude -p "what went wrong?"` sends the full log followed by your question. No copy-paste needed — data flows directly from source into Claude.

Context limits still apply. For a 50,000-line log, filter first — use `tail -100`, `grep ERROR`, or `head -500` before piping. Send the relevant slice, not the haystack.

terminology.definition: A Unix operator that sends output of one command as input to another. Connects programs like plumbing.
Standard input — the default source a program reads from. Piped data arrives through stdin.
The max input+output a model handles in one call. Piped content counts toward this.

codeExample.code:
# Pipe a file into Claude
cat README.md | claude -p "Summarize in 3 bullet points"

# Pipe git diff for review
git diff HEAD~1 | claude -p "Review these changes. Flag bugs."

# Pipe filtered logs
grep ERROR app.log | tail -50 | claude -p "Root cause?"

# Pipe curl output
curl -s https://api.example.com/status | claude -p "Is this healthy?"
codeExample.annotation: Each pipes from a different tool into Claude. git diff is especially useful — gives Claude exactly the changed code.

exercise.instruction: Pipe a text file into Claude with cat filename | claude -p "summarize this". Also pipe git log --oneline -10. Paste both commands and output.

content.explanation:
Now that Claude produces output non-interactively, it becomes a chain link. Put Claude between other commands. `git diff | claude -p "write a commit message" | git commit -F -`: diff flows in, commit message flows out, git commit receives it. Three commands, zero typing.

Use command substitution with `` to embed Claude output: `mkdir `. Claude suggests, jq extracts, mkdir creates. Anywhere you put static text, you can put Claude-generated text.

terminology.definition: Connecting commands with pipes so each output becomes the next input.
embeds one command output inside another. Inner runs first, output replaces the block.
A sequence of transformations — each step takes input, processes it, passes result forward.

codeExample.code:
# diff -> Claude -> commit
git diff --staged | claude -p "Write a concise commit message" | git commit -F -

# TODOs -> Claude -> prioritized
grep -rn "TODO" src/ | claude -p "Prioritize these TODOs"

# Command substitution
TARGET=Incomplete message. Which what?
echo "Entry point: "
codeExample.annotation: First automates commit messages. Second prioritizes TODOs. Third stores Claude answer in a variable.

content.explanation:
When many files need the same treatment, shell loops run Claude on every file automatically. A for loop iterates files and calls Claude once per file.

Two limits: use --max-turns 1 to prevent multi-turn reasoning. And add sleep 1 between iterations for rate limits. Test on 2-3 files before running on 200.

terminology.definition: Running the same operation on many items automatically.
Limits reasoning steps. 1 = one direct answer.
A restriction on API calls per time period.

codeExample.code:
mkdir -p summaries
for f in docs/*.md; do
  cat "" | claude -p "Summarize in 2 sentences" --max-turns 1 > "summaries/"""
  sleep 1
done
codeExample.annotation: Summarizes docs one by one with rate-limit sleep.

exercise.instruction: Write a for loop piping files into Claude with --max-turns 1. Paste loop + output.

title: CI/CD Integration — Claude in GitHub Actions

content.explanation:
CI/CD automatically runs tasks when you push code. GitHub Actions is the most common platform. Since Claude runs non-interactively, it fits into CI/CD pipelines.

Best use case: automated PR review. On pull request, a GitHub Action diffs against main, pipes into Claude, posts review as a PR comment.

Requires an Anthropic API key as a GitHub Actions secret. The --max-turns flag is critical — no looping endlessly in CI.

terminology.definition: Continuous Integration/Deployment — automated test, build, and deploy on every push.
GitHub automation platform. YAML workflows respond to events.
An encrypted variable in GitHub settings for safely passing API keys.

codeExample.code:
name: Claude PR Review
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]
jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
      - name: Review
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          git diff origin/main...HEAD | claude -p "Review this PR" --max-turns 1 --output-format json > review.json
codeExample.annotation: Triggers on PR. Diffs against main, Claude reviews, posts comment.

content.explanation:
Claude Code does not need your laptop. Run sessions on remote machines or schedule them like cron with AI. Nightly audits, weekly doc updates, daily log analysis while you sleep.

The /schedule command sets up recurring cloud tasks. Define what Claude does, when (cron syntax), which repo. Claude spins up a remote session, executes, you review later.

For one-off remote work, SSH into a server and run claude -p. Combine with tmux to keep sessions alive. Claude just needs a terminal and an API key.

terminology.definition: Scheduling format: minute, hour, day, month, weekday.
A Claude Code session on a server rather than local.
Claude Code command for recurring automated cloud tasks.

codeExample.code:
/schedule "Review src/" --cron "0 8 * * *" --repo my-org/my-project

ssh myserver "cd /app && claude -p \"Check logs\" --max-turns 1"
codeExample.annotation: /schedule for recurring tasks. SSH for remote execution.

exercise.instruction: Write a bash cron script: cd to project, pipe git log into Claude, save to weekly-summary.txt.

validation.errorMessage: Include cd, git log, pipe, redirect.

title: Capstone: Build an Automated Processing Pipeline

content.explanation:
Build a real shell pipeline using Claude as a processing step. Take real-world input, process through Claude, produce useful output.

Three stages: input collection, AI processing, output handling. Demonstrate at least two module techniques.

Project ideas: A) git changelog generator. B) code review script. C) doc freshness checker.

terminology.definition: Automated steps where data flows stage to stage.
First stage: gathering raw data.
Final stage: doing something useful with results.

codeExample.code:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
COMMITS=$(git log --since="1 week ago" --oneline --no-merges)
if [ -z "$COMMITS" ]; then echo "No commits."; exit 0; fi
CHANGELOG=$(echo "$COMMITS" | claude -p "Turn into changelog" --max-turns 1)
echo "# Changelog" > changelog.md
echo "$CHANGELOG" >> changelog.md
codeExample.annotation: Collects commits, Claude formats, writes changelog.

exercise.instruction: Build a pipeline with 3+ stages. Paste script and description.
